Written for a missionary anniversary rather than ordinary Sunday worship, the hymn was intended to connect the global spread of the gospel directly to justice for the vulnerable, not merely numerical growth -- "he shall come down like showers upon the fruitful earth," bringing "love, joy, hope, like flowers" wherever his reign takes root.

Montgomery, a lifelong campaigner against slavery and for social reform, wrote the hymn from the conviction that Christ's kingdom, once truly established among a people, could not leave unjust structures untouched -- a conviction that shaped much of his life outside hymn-writing as well.
family_story:
James Montgomery wrote "Hail to the Lord's Anointed" in 1821 for a meeting about sending missionaries around the world, based on Psalm 72 from the Old Testament.

The song describes Jesus as a king who cares especially about poor and needy people, and who brings fairness and kindness wherever he rules. Montgomery believed strongly that following Jesus should make the world more just, not just more religious.

title: O Happy Day
adult_story:
Philip Doddridge wrote "O Happy Day, That Fixed My Choice" to commemorate his own renewal of personal commitment to Christ, recorded in his private papers, drawing its rejoicing tone from Acts 8, where the Ethiopian official, having heard the gospel from Philip, immediately asks to be baptised and "went on his way rejoicing." Doddridge, an English Nonconformist minister and head of a dissenting academy that trained ministers outside the state church, died in 1751; the hymn was published posthumously in 1755 in a collection of his hymns.

Its refrain -- "happy day, happy day, when Jesus washed my sins away" -- was added later by an American editor and is not part of Doddridge's original text, but it captures accurately what Doddridge's verses already describe: a specific, remembered turning point rather than a vague general faith. The hymn treats the moment of committing to Christ as worth marking and remembering by name, not because of anything achieved on that day but because of what Christ had done in response to it.

It should not be confused with the very different, much later gospel song "Oh Happy Day," popularised by Edwin Hawkins in 1967 -- a distinct composition sharing only the phrase.
family_story:
Philip Doddridge was a minister a long time ago, in the 1700s. He wrote "O Happy Day" about a specific day he remembered clearly -- the day he committed his life to Jesus.

The song is based on a Bible story where a man immediately wanted to be baptised the moment he believed in Jesus, and went on his way full of joy. Doddridge's hymn celebrates that same kind of joyful, remembered moment of deciding to follow Christ.

(This isn't the same song as the more modern "Oh Happy Day" that many people know from the radio -- it's a much older hymn that just happens to share the same name.)

title: Hark the Glad Sound!
adult_story:
Philip Doddridge wrote "Hark the Glad Sound! The Saviour Comes" for Advent, likely around 1735, basing it directly on Luke 4, where Jesus reads aloud in the synagogue at Nazareth from Isaiah's prophecy and applies it to himself: sent "to preach the gospel to the poor... to heal the broken-hearted, to preach deliverance to the captives." Doddridge's hymn simply restates Christ's own description of his mission, verse by verse.

Each verse names a specific group Christ came for -- "the prisoner," "the mourning soul," "the humble poor" -- refusing to leave Christ's coming as an abstract theological event and insisting instead on concrete rescue for those who most needed it. Nothing in the hymn suggests these groups earned Christ's attention by their condition; rather, their very need is what his coming was for.

Published posthumously in 1755, four years after Doddridge's death, the hymn has remained a standard Advent hymn in English-language churches, its Isaiah-rooted language reminding congregations each December that Christ's first coming was announced, from his own lips, as good news specifically for those with nothing to offer in return.
family_story:
Philip Doddridge wrote "Hark the Glad Sound!" for Advent, the season before Christmas, based on a story in the Bible where Jesus read from an old prophecy about himself -- that he had come to help the poor, heal the brokenhearted, and set prisoners free.

The song says Jesus came especially for people who needed him most, not for people who thought they had everything figured out already. It's still sung in churches during Advent today.

title: O God of Bethel
adult_story:
Philip Doddridge wrote "O God of Bethel, By Whose Hand" based directly on Jacob's vow at Bethel in Genesis 28, where Jacob, fleeing his home with nothing, wakes from a dream of a ladder reaching to heaven and vows that if God will be with him and keep him, "then shall the Lord be my God."

Doddridge applied Jacob's vow to the ordinary congregation singing it, asking that the same God who had "led our fathers through the wilderness" would continue leading "this our pilgrim flock" and provide "food and raiment" as Jacob had asked. Rather than treating Jacob's story as ancient history only, the hymn treats every generation of believers as continuing the same journey of dependent trust that began at Bethel.

Published posthumously in 1755, it was later revised and expanded by the Scottish minister John Logan in 1781, and the combined version became widely used in Scottish and English Presbyterian worship, sung especially at the close of a service or year as a prayer for God's continuing guidance into whatever lay ahead.
family_story:
Philip Doddridge based "O God of Bethel" on an Old Testament story about Jacob, who ran away from home with nothing and had a dream about a ladder reaching up to heaven. Jacob promised that if God would take care of him, he would follow God for the rest of his life.

Doddridge's hymn asks God to keep guiding and providing for his people today, just as he guided Jacob long ago.

title: I Know That My Redeemer Lives
adult_story:
Samuel Medley, a former Royal Navy sailor who nearly lost his leg to a wound sustained in battle and who found genuine faith in Christ while recovering -- after his grandfather read him a sermon by Isaac Watts -- later became a Baptist pastor and hymn writer. He wrote "I Know That My Redeemer Lives" in 1775, taking his text directly from Job 19:25, one of the Old Testament's clearest statements of resurrection hope: "I know that my redeemer liveth, and that he shall stand at the latter day upon the earth."

Medley's hymn restates Job's ancient confidence as present-tense personal testimony, each verse repeating the refrain "he lives" as the ground for a different comfort -- "he lives to bless me with his love," "he lives to plead for me above," "he lives, all glory to his name." Written by a man whose own life had nearly ended at sea before Christ was made real to him, the hymn treats the risen, living Christ not as a doctrine to be affirmed only, but as a present help for exactly the fears and needs Medley names one by one.

It remains sung as a hymn of resurrection assurance, especially near Easter.
family_story:
Samuel Medley was a sailor who was badly wounded and thought he might lose his leg. While he was recovering, his grandfather read him a sermon, and Samuel became a Christian.

Later, he became a pastor and wrote "I Know That My Redeemer Lives," based on an old verse from the book of Job that says God's Redeemer is alive and will come again. The song says again and again, "He lives!" -- reminding everyone that Jesus isn't just a figure from history. He's alive right now.

title: Lizalis' idinga lakho (Fulfil Your Promise)
adult_story:
Tiyo Soga, the first Black South African to be ordained as a Presbyterian minister, wrote "Lizalis' idinga lakho" ("Fulfil Your Promise") in Xhosa in July 1857, on the ship carrying him and his new wife, Janet, back to South Africa after his theological training and ordination in Scotland. Soga was returning to a homeland recently devastated by the Xhosa Cattle-Killing movement, a catastrophic famine brought on the previous year after a prophecy that the ancestors would drive out European settlers if the Xhosa people destroyed their own cattle and crops.

Written into that specific context of national grief and uncertainty, the hymn calls on God to fulfil his covenant promises to his people, grounding present hope not in any prophecy or human plan but in God's own faithfulness. As the first widely used hymn written originally in Xhosa rather than translated into it, it gave southern African Christians a hymn of their own language and idiom rather than only imported European hymnody.

The hymn's significance outlasted Soga's own lifetime: it was sung in 1912 at the founding meeting of the South African Native National Congress, the organisation that later became the African National Congress, and has remained part of South African Christian and national memory since.
family_story:
Tiyo Soga was the first Black South African to become an ordained minister. In 1857, on the ship taking him and his new wife home from Scotland to South Africa, he wrote a hymn in his own language, Xhosa, called "Lizalis' idinga lakho," which means "Fulfil Your Promise."

He was returning to a country that had just been through a terrible famine. His hymn asked God to keep his promises to his people, even in hard times.

It became so important that it was sung more than fifty years later at a historic meeting that eventually led to the founding of the African National Congress.
